issues.original: Greedy is the algorithm paradigm that feels too good to be true: at every step, take the choice that looks best right now, never look back, and somehow end up at the global optimum. When it works, the code is almost embarrassingly short. When it doesn't, it produces confidently wrong answers — which is why the real skill is not writing greedy code, but recognising **when greedy is allowed**.
issues.fixed: Greedy is the algorithm paradigm that seems too good to be true: at every step, choose the option that looks best right now, never look back, and somehow reach the global optimum. When it works, the code is almost embarrassingly short. When it doesn't, it produces confidently wrong answers — which is why the real skill is recognizing when greedy is allowed.

issues.original: A **greedy algorithm** builds a solution incrementally by committing, at each step, to the choice that is locally best according to some criterion — and **never undoing it**. That last clause is what separates greedy from backtracking and from DP: there is no [CODE], no memo table of alternative branches.
issues.fixed: A **greedy algorithm** builds a solution incrementally by committing, at each step, to the choice that is locally best according to some criterion — and **never undoing it**. This is what distinguishes greedy from backtracking and dynamic programming: there is no [CODE], no memo table of alternative branches.

issues.original: For this strategy to be correct, the problem must have two structural properties: - **Greedy choice property.** There exists a locally optimal choice that is part of *some* globally optimal solution. (Equivalently: making this choice never destroys the best possible future.) - **Optimal substructure.** Once that choice is committed, the remainder of the problem is itself a smaller instance of the same problem, and an optimal solution for it combines with the greedy choice into an optimal solution for the original.
issues.fixed: For this strategy to be correct, the problem must have two structural properties: - **Greedy choice property.** There exists a locally optimal choice that is part of *some* globally optimal solution. (Equivalently: making this choice never ruins the best possible future.) - **Optimal substructure.** Once the choice is made, the remaining problem is a smaller instance of the same problem, and an optimal solution for it, combined with the greedy choice, forms an optimal solution for the original.

issues.original: The takeaway: **greedy trades correctness work for runtime**. You pay in proof effort and are rewarded with linear or near-linear time.
issues.fixed: The takeaway: **greedy trades correctness for runtime**. You pay in proof effort and are rewarded with linear or near-linear time.

issues.original: Coin change is the canonical cautionary tale. With the canonical US coin set [CODE], 'always take the largest coin that fits' produces an optimal answer for every amount. With a slightly different set [CODE] and amount [CODE], greedy picks [CODE] (three coins) while the optimal is [CODE] (two coins). Same algorithm, same intuition — but the second instance lacks the structural property (technically: the coin set is not a *matroid*) that makes greedy correct.
issues.fixed: Coin change is the canonical cautionary tale. With the standard US coin set [CODE], 'always take the largest coin that fits' produces an optimal answer for every amount. With a slightly different set [CODE] and amount [CODE], greedy picks [CODE] (three coins) while the optimal is [CODE] (two coins). The same algorithm and intuition apply, but the second instance lacks the structural property (technically: the coin set is not a *matroid*) that makes greedy correct.

issues.original: The lesson: **greedy correctness is a property of the problem, not the algorithm**. Always either prove it or recognise it from a known pattern.
issues.fixed: The lesson: **greedy correctness is a property of the problem, not the algorithm**. Always either prove it or recognize it from a known pattern.

issues.original: Most of the problems below are instances of one of these patterns. Recognising the pattern is 90% of the solving work.
issues.fixed: Most of the problems below are instances of one of these patterns. Recognizing the pattern is 90% of the solving work.

issues.original: The most common proof technique for greedy is the **exchange argument**: 1. Take any optimal solution [CODE]. 2. Show that you can replace the first non-greedy choice in [CODE] with the greedy choice, producing a new solution [CODE] that is **no worse**. 3. By induction, [CODE] can be transformed step by step into the greedy solution without losing optimality. 4. Therefore the greedy solution is itself optimal.
issues.fixed: The most common proof technique for greedy is the **exchange argument**: 1. Take any optimal solution [CODE]. 2. Show that you can replace the first non-greedy choice in [CODE] with the greedy choice, producing a new solution [CODE] that is **no worse**. 3. By induction, [CODE] can be transformed step by step into the greedy solution without losing optimality. 4. Therefore, the greedy solution is optimal.

issues.original: Backtracking is the algorithm you reach for whenever a problem asks you to *enumerate* something — every permutation, every subset, every legal board, every path through a grid. It is brute force with a brain: you build a candidate solution one decision at a time, abandon it the moment a constraint says "this cannot work", and undo your last move so the next branch sees a clean slate.
issues.fixed: Backtracking is the algorithm you use when a problem asks you to *enumerate* something — every permutation, every subset, every legal board, every path through a grid. It's brute force with a brain: you build a candidate solution one decision at a time, abandon it as soon as a constraint says "this won't work," and undo your last move so the next branch starts fresh.

issues.original:
The whole technique fits in three lines:

> **choose** -> **recurse** -> **un-choose**

That single rhythm solves Permutations, Combinations, Subsets, Word Search, N-Queens, and Sudoku, and it is the same template you will use on roughly 90% of "find all..." problems on LeetCode.
issues.fixed:
The whole technique fits in three lines:

> **choose** -> **recurse** -> **un-choose**

This simple pattern solves Permutations, Combinations, Subsets, Word Search, N-Queens, and Sudoku, and it's the same template you'll use for about 90% of "find all..." problems on LeetCode.

issues.original:
When to reach for backtracking:
- The problem says "return **all** ..." (permutations, combinations, subsets, partitions, parenthesizations).
- It is a constraint-satisfaction problem (N-Queens, Sudoku, graph coloring).
- The search space is exponential but most of it can be eliminated by checking partial constraints early.
- You need *one* solution and a witness, and a polynomial-time greedy / DP doesn't apply.
issues.fixed:
When to use backtracking:
- The problem asks to "return **all** ..." (permutations, combinations, subsets, partitions, parenthesizations).
- It's a constraint-satisfaction problem (N-Queens, Sudoku, graph coloring).
- The search space is exponential, but most of it can be eliminated by checking partial constraints early.
- You need *one* solution and a witness, and a polynomial-time greedy or DP approach doesn't apply.

issues.original:
When **not** to reach for it:
- You only need a count or an optimal value, and the subproblems overlap -> use DP.
- The search space is small enough that you can enumerate states iteratively.
- The problem reduces to BFS shortest path or a known graph algorithm.
issues.fixed:
When not to use it:
- You only need a count or an optimal value, and the subproblems overlap -> use DP.
- The search space is small enough to enumerate states iteratively.
- The problem reduces to BFS shortest path or a known graph algorithm.

issues.original: Every backtracking solution in this article is a thin specialization of one template. Memorize the shape; specialize the four slots.
issues.fixed: Every backtracking solution in this article is a slight variation of one template. Memorize the structure and adapt the four slots.

issues.original: DFS *traverses*: it visits each node once, never modifies what it walked over, and the recursion stack alone holds enough state. Backtracking *constructs*: the same [CODE] is mutated all the way down and restored all the way up, so the algorithm can reuse it across exponentially many branches without copying. Backtracking uses DFS as its motion, but adds the choose/un-choose discipline.
issues.fixed: DFS *traverses*: it visits each node once, never modifies what it walks over, and the recursion stack holds enough state. Backtracking *constructs*: the same [CODE] is mutated all the way down and restored all the way up, allowing the algorithm to reuse it across exponentially many branches without copying. Backtracking uses DFS for traversal but adds the choose/un-choose discipline.

issues.original: The goal of this article is not to give you yet another recitation of the standard template; it is to give you a **mental model** that explains why each template looks the way it does, and a small toolkit (three templates plus the answer-space pattern) that covers the vast majority of LeetCode problems.

Instead, it aims to provide a **mental model** that explains why each template looks the way it does, along with a small toolkit (three templates plus the answer-space pattern) that covers the vast majority of LeetCode problems.

issues.original: A binary tree problem is rarely about the tree. It is about *the order in which you touch nodes* and *what you remember from the children before deciding what to do at the parent*. Once those two ideas click, the four traversal orders, the iterative rewrites, the construction problems, and even classics like Validate BST and Maximum Depth all collapse into a handful of variations on the same recipe. This article builds that recipe end to end.
issues.fixed: A binary tree problem is rarely about the tree itself. It's about *the order in which you visit nodes* and *what you remember from the children before deciding what to do at the parent*. Once these two ideas click, the four traversal orders, iterative rewrites, construction problems, and even classics like Validate BST and Maximum Depth all boil down to variations on the same recipe. This article builds that recipe from start to finish.

issues.original: We use a single example tree throughout so the figures and the code line up exactly:
issues.fixed: We use a single example tree throughout, so the figures and code align perfectly.

issues.original: Its preorder is [CODE], inorder is [CODE], postorder is [CODE], and level-order is [CODE]. Keep this picture nearby; almost every figure below refers back to it.
issues.fixed: Its preorder is [CODE], inorder is [CODE], postorder is [CODE], and level-order is [CODE]. Keep this picture handy; most figures below refer back to it.

issues.original: A **binary tree** is a tree in which every node has at most two children, and crucially, the two children are *ordered* — left and right are not interchangeable. The empty tree counts as a binary tree, which is the base case that keeps every recursive definition clean.
issues.fixed: A **binary tree** is a tree where each node has at most two children, and the children are *ordered* — left and right are not interchangeable. An empty tree is also considered a binary tree, serving as the base case for recursive definitions.

issues.original: Three labels appear over and over in problem statements: - **Root** — the unique node with no parent. - **Internal node** — any node with at least one child. - **Leaf** — a node with no children. And two numbers describe how "tall" a node sits inside the tree: - The **depth** of a node is the number of edges from the root to it. The root has depth [CODE]. - The **height** of a node is the number of edges on the longest downward path to any leaf below it. A leaf has height [CODE]. The height of the *tree* is the height of the root.
issues.fixed: Three labels frequently appear in problem statements: - **Root** — the unique node with no parent. - **Internal node** — any node with at least one child. - **Leaf** — a node with no children. Two numbers describe a node's position in the tree: - The **depth** of a node is the number of edges from the root to it. The root has depth [CODE]. - The **height** of a node is the number of edges on the longest downward path to any leaf below it. A leaf has height [CODE]. The height of the *tree* is the height of the root.

issues.original: The minimal Python representation is a class with a value and two child pointers: That is the entire data model. Everything below is some way of walking it.
issues.fixed: The minimal Python representation is a class with a value and two child pointers. This is the entire data model. Everything below is a way of traversing it.

issues.original: A few special shapes show up as constraints in problem statements; recognising them often unlocks an asymptotically better solution. - **Full binary tree** — every node has either [CODE] or [CODE] children, never exactly one. Common in expression trees. - **Complete binary tree** — every level is full except possibly the last, which fills from left to right. Heaps are stored this way, which lets you implement them in a flat array. - **Balanced binary tree** — for every node, the heights of the two subtrees differ by at most one. This is the property AVL and red-black trees maintain to keep operations [FORMULA]. - **Binary search tree (BST)** — for every node, *every* value in the left subtree is strictly less than the node and *every* value in the right subtree is strictly greater. We will exploit this directly when validating a BST.
issues.fixed: A few special shapes often appear as constraints in problem statements; recognizing them can lead to more efficient solutions. - **Full binary tree** — every node has either [CODE] or [CODE] children, never exactly one. Common in expression trees. - **Complete binary tree** — every level is full except possibly the last, which fills from left to right. Heaps are stored this way, allowing implementation in a flat array. - **Balanced binary tree** — for every node, the heights of the two subtrees differ by at most one. This is the property maintained by AVL and red-black trees to keep operations [FORMULA]. - **Binary search tree (BST)** — for every node, all values in the left subtree are strictly less than the node, and all values in the right subtree are strictly greater. We will use this property directly when validating a BST.

issues.original: Every depth-first traversal performs three actions at each node — visit the node, recurse left, recurse right — but in different orders. The order is what gives the traversal its name: | Order | Action sequence | Resulting visit list on our tree | | --- | --- | --- | | **Preorder** | root → left → right | [CODE] | | **Inorder** | left → root → right | [CODE] | | **Postorder** | left → right → root | [CODE] | Figure 2 shows all three on the same tree, with each node numbered by its visit step. The "where does the root sit" rule is the entire trick: the root is processed *first* in preorder, *between* the two children in inorder, and *last* in postorder. The recursive code makes this almost too obvious:
issues.fixed: Every depth-first traversal performs three actions at each node — visit the node, recurse left, and recurse right — but in different orders. The order gives the traversal its name: | Order | Action sequence | Resulting visit list on our tree | | --- | --- | --- | | **Preorder** | root → left → right | [CODE] | | **Inorder** | left → root → right | [CODE] | | **Postorder** | left → right → root | [CODE] | Figure 2 shows all three on the same tree, with each node numbered by its visit step. The key is where the root is processed: *first* in preorder, *between* the two children in inorder, and *last* in postorder. The recursive code makes this clear.

issues.original: The choice of order is dictated by *when you have the information you need*. Preorder is right when a parent decision must happen before its children — copying a tree, printing a directory tree, encoding for serialization. Inorder is right whenever the BST ordering matters — sorted output, validation, the [FORMULA]-th smallest. Postorder is right when a parent's answer is built from its children's answers — deleting a tree, computing subtree sums or sizes, and (as we'll see) computing height.
issues.fixed: The choice of order depends on *when you need the information*. Preorder is used when a parent decision must happen before its children, such as copying a tree, printing a directory tree, or encoding for serialization. Inorder is used when the BST ordering matters, like for sorted output, validation, or finding the [FORMULA]-th smallest element. Postorder is used when a parent's answer is built from its children's answers, such as deleting a tree, computing subtree sums or sizes, and (as we'll see) computing height.

issues.original: Inorder is the most useful of the three to study iteratively, because the iterative version reveals exactly what the call stack was doing for you. **Recursive.** The "left, root, right" pattern straight from the definition: Time is [FORMULA] — every node is touched once. Space is [FORMULA] for the call stack, where [FORMULA] is the tree height; that is [FORMULA] for a balanced tree and [FORMULA] for a degenerate "linked-list-shaped" tree. **Iterative.** The recursive call stack is doing two things: it remembers the *path of ancestors* on the way down, and it *resumes* each ancestor after its left subtree is done. Both jobs can be performed by an explicit stack: The two phases mirror the two halves of the recursive call: pushing onto the stack stands in for "going down a recursive call", and popping stands in for "returning from one". Figure 4 puts the two side by side, frame by frame, on the running tree — they really are the same algorithm under different syntax. Two pitfalls trip people up here. First, forgetting [CODE] after popping creates an infinite loop, because the same node is pushed and popped forever. Second, treating the inner [CODE] as [CODE] only descends one step at a time and misses the leftmost grandchildren; you must drain the

issues.original: If you have ever caught yourself writing a double [CODE] loop to inspect every contiguous subarray, **sliding window** is probably the optimisation you are missing. It turns an [FORMULA] or [FORMULA] scan into a single linear pass by *reusing the work* it has already done.

issues.original: A sliding window is a contiguous range [CODE] over an array or string. Instead of recomputing everything when the range moves, we **add the element entering on the right** and **remove the element leaving on the left**. Each element is touched at most twice, so the total cost is [FORMULA].

Instead of recomputing everything when the range moves, we **add the element entering on the right** and **remove the element leaving on the left**. Each element is touched at most twice, making the total cost [FORMULA].

issues.original: There are two flavours: - **Fixed-size window** — width is a constant [CODE] and both pointers advance together. - **Variable-size window** — [CODE] expands to grow the window, [CODE] contracts to repair it when an invariant breaks.
issues.fixed: There are two flavors: - **Fixed-size window** — the width is a constant [CODE], and both pointers advance together. - **Variable-size window** — [CODE] expands to grow the window, and [CODE] contracts to repair it when an invariant breaks.

issues.original: The orange cell is *entering* the window; the pink one is *leaving*. Notice the right column: each step is one subtraction and one addition, never a fresh [CODE] call. That is the entire trick.
issues.fixed: The orange cell is *entering* the window; the pink one is *leaving*. In the right column, each step involves one subtraction and one addition, never a fresh [CODE] call. That's the entire trick.

issues.original: When the window size is not given, we use two pointers and let the problem dictate the policy: - **Find the longest valid window:** keep expanding [CODE]; whenever the window goes invalid, contract [CODE] until it is valid again, then record the length. - **Find the shortest valid window:** keep expanding [CODE] until the window becomes valid; then contract [CODE] as far as possible while staying valid, recording the length on each shrink.

issues.original: Stacks and queues look unassuming next to graphs or DP, but they sit underneath an astonishing fraction of interview problems. The reason is simple: most algorithmic questions are really questions about *order of access*. Stacks give you LIFO (last in, first out); queues give you FIFO (first in, first out); and once you add the variants — monotonic stack, deque, priority queue — you have efficient answers for bracket matching, next-greater-element, sliding-window extrema, top-K, BFS, and a long tail of 'implement X using Y' puzzles.
issues.fixed: Stacks and queues may seem unassuming compared to graphs or dynamic programming, but they underpin a surprising number of interview problems. The reason is straightforward: most algorithmic questions boil down to *order of access*. Stacks provide LIFO (last in, first out), while queues offer FIFO (first in, first out). Add variants like monotonic stacks, deques, and priority queues, and you can efficiently solve problems like bracket matching, next-greater-element, sliding-window extrema, top-K, BFS, and a host of 'implement X using Y' puzzles.

issues.original: This part of the series walks the whole landscape end-to-end. We start from the bare data structures, then work through six representative LeetCode problems with full traces, and finish with a comparison table and a Q&A that targets the mistakes I see candidates make most often.
issues.fixed: This part of the series covers the entire landscape. We start with the basic data structures, then work through six representative LeetCode problems with full traces, and conclude with a comparison table and a Q&A addressing common mistakes I see candidates make.

issues.original: The key observation is the *nesting* rule: when a closing bracket arrives, it must match the **most recently opened** one that is still unclosed. 'Most recently opened' is exactly what a stack tracks.
issues.fixed: The key observation is the *nesting* rule: a closing bracket must match the **most recently opened** one that is still unclosed. This is exactly what a stack tracks.

issues.original: Hash tables buy you speed by spending memory. Two pointers is the opposite trade: spend a little structural assumption — the array is sorted, the list might have a cycle, the answer lives in a contiguous window — and you get [FORMULA] time with [FORMULA] extra space.
issues.fixed: Hash tables buy you speed by using more memory. Two pointers, on the other hand, trade a bit of structural assumption — like a sorted array, a list with a cycle, or an answer in a contiguous window — for [FORMULA] time with [FORMULA] extra space.

issues.original: The pattern looks trivial in code (two indices and a [CODE] loop) but it has more failure modes than any other beginner technique: off-by-one indices, infinite loops, missed duplicates, wrong pointer moved on tie. The cure is to think in **invariants** rather than in moves.
issues.fixed: The pattern seems simple in code (two indices and a [CODE] loop), but it has more failure modes than any other beginner technique: off-by-one errors, infinite loops, missed duplicates, and moving the wrong pointer on a tie. The solution is to think in terms of **invariants** rather than moves.

issues.original: This article walks through four flavors of two-pointer thinking — **collision**, **fast/slow**, **sliding window**, and **partition** — and pins each one to a problem that you will see on a real interview: Two Sum II, 3Sum, Container With Most Water, Linked List Cycle, and Move Zeroes.
